summary: My mom was born in Kham, Tibet in 1969, a place that holds her early memories and childhood. It was from Kham that she was forced to flee in 1985 when the Chinese Communist Party invaded Tibet. Leaving Kham was terrifying for her because she had never been away from her family or home before. This place represents the beginning of her journey and the life she had to leave behind.

Tibet is where my mom's story begins, and it was her homeland until the Chinese Communist Party invaded in 1985. The occupation took away the freedom of the Tibetan people, forcing her to escape by foot to India. Even after moving to the United States, she never forgot Tibet and remained deeply connected to its struggle. She actively supports the Free Tibet movement and has risked her life to raise awareness about the injustice happening there.

The Chinese Communist Party invaded Tibet in 1985, which changed my mom’s life forever. Their occupation took away the freedom of the Tibetan people and forced her to flee her home in Kham. The fear of punishment from the Chinese government kept her family behind while she escaped alone. This organization is the reason she had to endure such a dangerous journey and fight for Tibetan rights throughout her life.

After fleeing Tibet, my mom traveled by foot to India, where she lived and worked for many years. India was a place of refuge but also a place where she had to grow up fast and learn to be independent. It was in India that she waited until she won the lottery under the Immigration Act of 1990, which allowed her to move to America. India was both a safe haven and a stepping stone in her long journey.

The Immigration Act of 1990 was a turning point for my mom because it allowed 1,000 Tibetan refugees, including her, to come to America. She won the lottery under this act, which gave her the opportunity to leave India and start a new life in the United States. This event was crucial in her journey, as it opened the door to safety and a better future. Without it, she might have remained stuck in India indefinitely.

When my mom finally moved to the United States, she faced many challenges like learning a new language and adapting to a different lifestyle. She had help from a sponsor who guided her through the transition, but it was still a difficult adjustment. Despite this, she built a new life, made friends, and continued to support the Tibetan cause from afar. America became a place of hope and new beginnings for her and our family.

My mom has been an active supporter of the Free Tibet movement since coming to America. She believes deeply in fighting for the rights and freedom of the Tibetan people. In 1990, she even risked her life by doing an 11-day hunger strike to raise awareness to the United Nations about the injustices in Tibet. This movement is a cause close to her heart and a way she continues to honor her homeland and family.

In 1990, my mom participated in a hunger strike for 11 days to bring attention to the United Nations about the injustices happening in Tibet. She wanted the world to see the suffering caused by the Chinese Communist Party’s occupation. The United Nations represented a global platform where she hoped to make a difference for her people. This act showed her courage and commitment to the Tibetan cause.
